Emails with TechServices password-reset links
- From: support@techservices.com
  - Subject: TechServices Password Reset Request
  - Received: 2024-05-19 23:50:00
  - Read: no (unread)
  - Body contains this reset link:
    https://techservices.com/auth/password-reset/token=?asfbuy3y2cdaqhvei

- From: tech-services-password@aol.com (suspicious)
  - Subject: Your TechServices password reset request
  - Received: 2024-05-19 23:55:00
  - Read: yes
  - Body contains two links:
    - https://www.password-reset-12bh12.com  (likely a phishing site)
    - https://techservices.com/auth/password-reset/token=?fjahwb24jab3

Security note and recommended next steps
- The support@techservices.com message appears to be the official reset email (and is unread). The aol.com message looks suspicious — it contains a clearly non-official domain and is likely phishing even though it also repeats a techservices.com-looking link.
- Only use the link from the official TechServices message (support@techservices.com) if you initiated the reset. If you did not request a reset, do not click any links.
- If you’re unsure, I recommend contacting Blue Sparrow Tech IT/Security or TechServices support directly (not via links in the suspicious email). You can also:
  - Mark/delete the suspicious AOL message and keep the official one, or
  - Forward the suspicious message to IT/security for analysis.

What would you like me to do next?
- Open or forward either email, delete the suspicious message, mark it as phishing, or send a message to IT/security on your behalf. Or, if you want, I can click the official reset link for you and walk you through the reset steps (you’ll need to complete any verification).
